key_findings.prevalence_trend: Age-standardized prevalence decreased from 26.0% (1998) to 21.8% (2021). Crude prevalence showed more variation but remained 25-30% range from 2010-2021.
key_findings.awareness_trend: Awareness improved dramatically from 23.5% (1998) to 74.1% (2021), with major gains between 1998-2009, then plateau 2009-2021.
key_findings.treatment_trend: Treatment improved from 20.4% (1998) to 70.3% (2021), following similar pattern to awareness.
key_findings.control_trend: Control improved approximately 10-fold from 4.9% (1998) to 56.0% (2021), most rapid improvement 1998-2009.
key_findings.gender_differences: Age-standardized rates generally higher in men than women across most years.
key_findings.pandemic_effect: Slight increase in prevalence during 2020 (29.4% crude), with increasing slope slowing during pandemic period.

all_goals_met.comment: All three targets: HbA1c <6.5%, BP <140/85, LDL-C <100

key_findings.prevalence_trend: Steady increase from 6.71% (1998-2005) to 15.61% (2020-2022), with acceleration after 2010. COVID-19 spike in 2020 (16.7%), then slight decrease.
key_findings.awareness_trend: Gradual improvement from 62.5% (2013) to 74.7% (2021-2022), with nadir at 64.37% (2013-2015).
key_findings.control_trend: HbA1c <6.5% control rates variable: 24.8-28.3% (2013-2018), dipped to 24.5% (2019-2020), recovered to 32.4% (2021-2022).
key_findings.integrated_management: Only 15.9% meet all three integrated targets (HbA1c, BP, LDL-C), highlighting treatment gap.
key_findings.pandemic_effect: Prevalence spike in 2020 (16.7%) followed by stabilization. Lipid control worsened during 2019-2020.
key_findings.gender_differences: Men have higher prevalence (18.1%) than women (13.0%) in 2021-2022.

control_among_prevalence.note: Control defined as cholesterol <200 mg/dL or on treatment with good control

control_among_treatment.2024_note: 87% of lipid-lowering drug users maintain cholesterol <200 mg/dL

key_findings.prevalence_trend: Steady increase from 41.30% (2005-2009) to 48.41% (2020-2022). Hypercholesterolemia specifically increased from 8.8% (2007) to 22.4% (2022).
key_findings.awareness_trend: Dramatic improvement from 17.87% (2005-2009) to 48.90% (2020-2022), tripling over period.
key_findings.treatment_trend: Significant increase from 7.10% (2005-2009) to 38.19% (2020-2022). Medication use among untreated increased from 1.6-1.5% (2007-2009) to 4.7-7% (2013-2015).
key_findings.control_trend: Among treated, control improved from 52.55% (2005-2009) to 74.55% (2020-2022), with 87% achieving target in 2024.
key_findings.pandemic_acceleration: Awareness, treatment, and control increased more rapidly during 2020-2022 pandemic period.
key_findings.lipid_level_changes: Total cholesterol increased slightly 2005-2015; HDL cholesterol improved substantially (men +5.2, women +7.5 mg/dL), indicating favorable lipid profile shifts.
key_findings.gender_differences: Women historically had lower dyslipidemia prevalence (31.3% vs 45.6% in 2018), but prevalence of dyslipidemia type 2 similar between genders.
key_findings.awareness_gap: One-third of adults with hypercholesterolemia remain unaware of condition (2024).

metadata.data_sources: Korean National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (KNHANES), National Health Insurance Big Data, Korean Society of Hypertension, Korean Diabetes Association, Korean Society of Lipid and Atherosclerosis
data_quality_notes: Hypertension: Comprehensive individual year prevalence data 1998-2021. Management metrics available primarily for recent years and key periods.
Diabetes: Preference for period aggregates (3-year rolling periods) in source documents. Prevalence appears to have peaked in 2020 (16.7%), likely due to COVID-19, then stabilized.
Dyslipidemia: Data primarily in period aggregates (3-5 year periods). 2007 represents earliest year with standardized hypercholesterolemia measure.
Pandemic effects: Notable increases or changes in 2020 across all three conditions, followed by varying trajectories 2020-2022.
Gender stratification: All conditions show male predominance in age-standardized prevalence.
